tips.short: One prompt, one job. Split anything bigger.
tips.heading: Task isolation
tips.what: Each prompt should ask for exactly one thing. If you need three outputs, write three prompts or a chain.
tips.when: Your prompt is getting long and outputs still feel scattered. You're asking Claude to write, review, and refactor all in one go.
tips.why: Every added task pulls focus from the others. A 900-word prompt that mixes tasks usually fails not because it's long, but because it's divided.

tips.short: Tell Claude exactly what the output should look like.
tips.heading: Explicit output format
tips.what: Specify structure, length, and format before you ask for content. For JSON: provide the exact schema. For prose: say how many paragraphs. For code: name the function signature.
tips.when: You're getting inconsistent structure across runs. The output is right but in the wrong shape.
tips.why: 'Respond in JSON' leaves the structure undefined. 'Respond as {name: string, score: number}' does not.

tips.short: Give Claude a role before the task, not after.
tips.heading: Role prompting
tips.what: Open with 'You are a [role] with [specific expertise].' Put it in the system prompt if you're using one. It sets the frame for everything that follows.
tips.when: You want a specific perspective, tone, or knowledge base. Especially useful for technical reviews, legal summaries, or domain-specific writing.
tips.why: A role anchors vocabulary, assumptions, and the level of detail Claude reaches for. Without one, it defaults to a general assistant voice.

tips.short: Show 2-3 examples for edge cases, not just the happy path.
tips.heading: Few-shot examples
tips.what: Add 2-3 input/output pairs that show how edge cases should be handled. Put them directly in the prompt before the actual task.
tips.when: Your prompt works on simple cases but breaks on anything unusual. You've tried rewriting the instructions and it still doesn't catch it.
tips.why: The model learns from what you demonstrate, not just what you describe. Examples that cover the edges define behavior more precisely than any instruction can.

tips.short: Ask Claude to reason before it answers.
tips.heading: Chain-of-thought prompting
tips.what: Add 'Think step by step before answering' or 'Show your reasoning first.' For structured output, ask for a thinking section before the final answer.
tips.when: Multi-step problems, math, logic, anything where the first answer tends to be wrong. Complex decisions with trade-offs.
tips.why: Forcing reasoning before output catches errors the model would otherwise commit to silently. The reasoning is visible, so you can spot where it went wrong.

tips.short: Specify what NOT to do — it's often clearer than what to do.
tips.heading: Negative constraints
tips.what: Add explicit 'Do NOT' lines: 'Do not add explanatory comments. Do not use em dashes. Do not suggest alternatives unless asked.'
tips.when: Claude keeps adding things you didn't ask for. Or keeps omitting things despite your instructions.
tips.why: Positive instructions leave gaps. Negative constraints close the specific gaps that your use case keeps falling into.

tips.short: Use XML tags to separate sections in complex prompts.
tips.heading: Structured prompt sections with XML
tips.what: Wrap distinct sections in tags: context, task, examples, constraints, output_format. Claude parses these cleanly and rarely mixes them up.
tips.when: Prompts over 400 words where different sections serve different purposes. System prompts with multiple moving parts.
tips.why: Prose prompts blur where context ends and instructions begin. Tags make structure explicit and reduce ambiguity at the boundaries.

tips.short: Use Claude to improve your prompts before using them.
tips.heading: Meta-prompting
tips.what: Share your prompt and ask: 'What ambiguities do you see in this? What edge cases does it not handle? How would you rewrite it to be more precise?' Then use the improved version.
tips.when: A prompt has been underperforming and you've already tried rewriting it yourself. Before building a production prompt into a workflow.
tips.why: Claude can see holes in instructions that you can't spot because you know what you meant. It's faster than trial and error.

tips.short: Ask Claude to attack its own output before you trust it.
tips.heading: Adversarial self-review
tips.what: After getting an output, ask: 'What are the weakest parts of this? What would a critic say? What assumptions might be wrong?' Then decide whether to revise.
tips.when: High-stakes outputs: code going to production, content going to stakeholders, decisions with real consequences.
tips.why: Claude is more likely to spot its own errors when explicitly asked to look for them than when asked to confirm they don't exist.

tips.short: Turn on extended thinking for hard problems that need real reasoning.
tips.heading: Extended thinking mode
tips.what: Switch to Opus and enable extended thinking. Claude spends more tokens on internal reasoning before answering. Slower and costs more — use it selectively.
tips.when: Complex architecture decisions, hard debugging, mathematical reasoning, anything where the first answer has been consistently wrong.
tips.why: Regular mode gives you the statistically likely answer fast. Extended thinking gives you a more carefully worked-through one. Different tool, different price.

tips.short: Reset context when you switch tasks.
tips.heading: /clear between tasks
tips.what: /clear wipes the conversation. Use it when you're done with one task and starting something unrelated.
tips.when: Moving from debugging to feature work. Jumping to a different codebase area. Starting fresh after a long session.
tips.why: Old context from a different task bleeds into the current one. /clear stops that.

tips.short: Write instructions once. Claude reads them at every session start.
tips.heading: CLAUDE.md for persistent context
tips.what: A CLAUDE.md file at your project root gets read automatically whenever Claude starts. Architecture notes, conventions, constraints — put it all there.
tips.when: Any project you return to more than twice. Stop re-explaining your setup.
tips.why: Every time you re-explain your stack or preferences, that's time you're not getting back. Write it once.

tips.short: Point Claude directly at a file instead of describing it.
tips.heading: File references with @
tips.what: Type @filename anywhere in your message. Claude finds the file and reads it.
tips.when: Reviewing a specific file, asking about a component, comparing two implementations.
tips.why: Describing a file takes words and introduces ambiguity. @filename just gets there.

tips.short: Generate a CLAUDE.md for a new project automatically.
tips.heading: /init command
tips.what: /init scans your project and writes a CLAUDE.md with detected stack, conventions, and structure. Edit it after to add what it missed.
tips.when: Starting on a new codebase. Before your first real session on a project.
tips.why: Manual CLAUDE.md creation is slow. /init gives you 80% of the file in seconds — you just fill in the gaps.

tips.short: Ask a side question mid-task without opening a new thread.
tips.heading: Side questions without losing your place
tips.what: Type /btw followed by your question. Claude answers inline, then picks up where you left off.
tips.when: You need a quick fact mid-refactor. You want to check syntax without starting a new conversation.
tips.why: Opening a new thread means re-explaining everything. /btw skips that.

tips.short: Summarize a long session and keep working without losing context.
tips.heading: /compact to extend sessions
tips.what: /compact summarizes the conversation into a compact form, freeing up context window space. You keep working without starting over.
tips.when: A long session is getting slow or the context warning appears. Long refactors, debugging sessions that have covered a lot of ground.
tips.why: Hitting the context limit forces a full reset. /compact lets you keep going while preserving the important decisions.

tips.short: Pick up exactly where you left off after closing Claude.
tips.heading: /resume command
tips.what: /resume restores your previous session context, including what you were working on and what decisions were made.
tips.when: Coming back to a task after a break or across days. When you want continuity without re-explaining.
tips.why: Starting cold means re-establishing context every time. /resume removes that overhead.

tips.short: Save facts that carry over into future conversations.
tips.heading: Persistent memory
tips.what: Use /memory to store things Claude should always know: your role, preferences, project context. It recalls them automatically.
tips.when: Anything you've explained more than twice. Stack preferences, shortcuts, recurring project background.
tips.why: You stop repeating yourself. That's it.

tips.short: Decide what goes into the context window before you start, not during.
tips.heading: Context window planning
tips.what: Before a big task, explicitly list what context you need: which files, which history, which constraints. Load only that.
tips.when: Complex multi-file tasks. Anything where you've had Claude lose focus mid-session before.
tips.why: Unplanned context accumulates noise. Every irrelevant file or stale turn is competing with what you actually need.

tips.short: Map an unfamiliar codebase before touching it.
tips.heading: /gsd-map-codebase skill
tips.what: Runs parallel mapper agents across your codebase and writes structured analysis to .planning/codebase/. Covers tech, architecture, quality, and concerns.
tips.when: Onboarding to a new repo. Before planning a large refactor. When Claude keeps misunderstanding how the code is organized.
tips.why: Exploring a codebase file by file is slow and misses the structure. A map gives Claude (and you) a shared mental model before the work starts.

tips.short: Remove AI writing patterns before sharing anything.
tips.heading: Humanizer skill
tips.what: The humanizer skill scans for em dash overuse, rule-of-three, vague attributions, 'vibrant/pivotal/leverage', sycophantic openers, and rewrites them out.
tips.when: Before sharing any AI-generated prose externally. Emails, docs, reports, client-facing content.
tips.why: AI writing patterns are recognizable. They undermine credibility in professional contexts even when the content is correct.

tips.short: Get a weighted quality score for your codebase.
tips.heading: Health check skill
tips.what: The health skill produces a composite 0-10 score across test coverage, code complexity, duplication, dependency health, and documentation. Shows trends over time.
tips.when: Before major releases. When the codebase feels like it's getting harder to work in. After a fast sprint.
tips.why: Code quality degrades gradually. A score gives you something concrete to track and defend against.

tips.short: Structured review for correctness, security, and edge cases.
tips.heading: Code review skill
tips.what: Runs a structured review covering: correctness, edge cases, error handling, security vulnerabilities, performance, and code style. Produces a ranked findings list.
tips.when: Before merging any non-trivial PR. After Claude writes a significant chunk of code.
tips.why: AI-generated code often passes lint and tests but misses edge cases or security issues that a second pass catches.

tips.short: Never say 'done' without showing evidence it works.
tips.heading: Verification before completion
tips.what: No task is complete until you can show a test passing, a screenshot, or a successful run. Not 'it should work' — proof.
tips.when: Always. Especially when Claude says 'I've implemented X' — ask to see it run.
tips.why: Confident-sounding wrong answers are the most expensive kind. Evidence is cheap.

tips.short: Write the failing test before writing the implementation.
tips.heading: Test-driven development
tips.what: Write the test, watch it fail (confirm it fails for the right reason), then write the implementation to make it pass.
tips.when: Any feature or function with clear expected behavior. Especially useful for bug fixes — write a test that reproduces the bug before fixing it.
tips.why: Tests written after the fact often test what the code does, not what it should do. Tests written first test intent.

tips.short: Ask Claude to score its own output 1-10 and explain why.
tips.heading: LLM-as-judge pattern
tips.what: After getting an output, ask: 'Rate this output from 1-10 on [criteria]. Explain each deduction.' Use the explanation to iterate.
tips.when: Writing tasks, code quality assessments, anything where you want to surface latent issues without reading the whole output yourself.
tips.why: Claude can apply criteria more consistently than human spot-checks, especially across large outputs.

tips.short: Screenshot your app after every deploy to catch regressions.
tips.heading: Canary post-deploy monitoring
tips.what: The canary skill takes screenshots of key pages, compares against a baseline, measures performance, and alerts on anomalies. Runs after deploy.
tips.when: After every production deploy. Before and after any frontend change that touches layout.
tips.why: Broken deploys that look fine to CI but break visually go unnoticed until users report them. Canary catches them first.

tips.short: Refactor one thing at a time — no behavior change, ever.
tips.heading: Refactor skill
tips.what: The refactor skill enforces safe refactoring: one change at a time, tests must pass before and after, no behavior changes allowed in the same commit as structural changes.
tips.when: Cleaning up after a fast sprint. Improving code that works but is hard to read or extend.
tips.why: Refactors that mix structural and behavioral changes are where bugs hide. The discipline of keeping them separate makes regressions obvious.

tips.short: Define what 'good' looks like before you generate.
tips.heading: Upfront evaluation criteria
tips.what: Before asking for output, write 3-5 criteria for what a successful output looks like. Share them in the prompt. Then score the output against them.
tips.when: Any high-stakes generation: architecture decisions, marketing copy, code that will be reviewed by others.
tips.why: Without criteria, you're judging outputs by feeling. With criteria, you're judging them by standards you can also share with collaborators.

tips.short: Track Core Web Vitals and bundle size across deploys.
tips.heading: Benchmark skill
tips.what: The benchmark skill measures performance metrics (LCP, CLS, FID, bundle sizes) and compares against previous runs to catch regressions before they reach users.
tips.when: After any frontend change. Before shipping a new feature. As part of CI.
tips.why: Performance regressions are invisible until they're bad. Benchmarks make them visible at the commit level.

tips.short: Get a warning before any destructive command runs.
tips.heading: Careful skill
tips.what: The careful skill intercepts potentially destructive commands (rm -rf, DROP TABLE, git reset --hard, force-push) and asks for explicit confirmation before proceeding.
tips.when: Any session involving database operations, git history changes, or file deletions. Keep it on by default.
tips.why: Destructive commands that run silently are unrecoverable. One confirmation step costs nothing and saves everything.

tips.short: Never fix what you haven't confirmed you can reproduce.
tips.heading: Reproduce before fixing
tips.what: Before writing any fix, write a minimal reproduction: the exact input, state, and steps that cause the failure. Confirm it fails consistently.
tips.when: Every bug. Always. Even the obvious ones.
tips.why: Fixes for bugs you can't reproduce often fix the wrong thing, or fix nothing and appear to work.

tips.short: Paste the full error — not a summary of it.
tips.heading: Complete error context
tips.what: When sharing an error with Claude, paste the full stack trace, the command that triggered it, and any relevant log lines around it.
tips.when: Every debugging session. Resist the urge to summarize or paraphrase the error.
tips.why: Summaries lose the specific details (line numbers, internal function names, exact error types) that distinguish the real cause from the apparent one.

tips.short: Is the server running? Is the env var set? Check first.
tips.heading: Obvious checks before deep debugging
tips.what: Before any deep investigation: is the process running? Are environment variables set correctly? Did the last deploy actually complete? Are you looking at the right environment?
tips.when: First 2 minutes of any debugging session.
tips.why: The most embarrassing debugging sessions end with a missing .env file. The obvious checks take 30 seconds and are wrong surprisingly often.

tips.short: 4-phase systematic debugging: reproduce, isolate, hypothesize, fix.
tips.heading: Investigate skill
tips.what: The investigate skill enforces an iron law: no fix before root cause. Phases: (1) reproduce, (2) isolate to smallest failing case, (3) form hypotheses ranked by probability, (4) test the highest-probability hypothesis first.
tips.when: Bugs you can't immediately explain. Intermittent failures. Anything you've 'fixed' before and it came back.
tips.why: Random fixes for unexplained bugs create new bugs. Systematic investigation finds the actual cause.

tips.short: Persistent debugging state that survives context resets.
tips.heading: /gsd-debug skill
tips.what: The gsd-debug skill maintains a structured debugging session across context resets. It saves hypotheses, tried solutions, and current state so you can pick up where you left off.
tips.when: Hard bugs that take more than one session. Complex multi-system issues where you need to track what you've ruled out.
tips.why: Starting a debugging session from scratch after a context reset is expensive. Persisted state means you don't re-investigate what you already ruled out.

tips.short: The first fix that makes the error go away isn't always the right fix.
tips.heading: Root cause vs symptom
tips.what: When a fix works, ask: 'Is this fixing the root cause or suppressing the symptom?' A try/catch that hides an exception is not a fix. A null check that prevents a crash may not address why null appears.
tips.when: After any fix that feels too easy. Whenever you're tempted to add a guard clause without understanding why the condition occurs.
tips.why: Symptom fixes accumulate as technical debt. Root cause fixes prevent the symptom from recurring in other places.

tips.short: Full OWASP review: auth, injection, credentials, XSS, path traversal.
tips.heading: Security audit skill
tips.what: Runs a structured security review covering OWASP Top 10: SQL/command injection, auth bypass, credential exposure, XSS, path traversal, insecure dependencies, and more.
tips.when: Before any release with auth changes. When adding new API endpoints. After adding new dependencies.
tips.why: Security issues found in review cost hours. Security issues found in production cost careers.

tips.short: STRIDE threat modeling, secrets archaeology, and supply chain review.
tips.heading: Chief Security Officer skill
tips.what: The cso skill runs a deep security posture review: STRIDE threat model, hardcoded secrets scan, supply chain risk, privilege escalation vectors, and defense-in-depth gaps.
tips.when: Before launching to production. When security requirements are formal (SOC2, GDPR, etc.). After a security incident.
tips.why: OWASP catches common vulnerabilities. CSO-level review catches architectural and systemic risk patterns that individual checks miss.

tips.short: Treat user input as untrusted — even when it's going to an LLM.
tips.heading: Prompt injection awareness
tips.what: Prompt injection is when user-supplied content manipulates the LLM's behavior. Sanitize inputs, separate system and user content clearly, and never trust LLM output as code without review.
tips.when: Building any system where user input reaches an LLM prompt. Especially RAG systems, chatbots, and agentic workflows.
tips.why: Injection attacks on LLMs are the new SQL injection. They're real, being exploited, and most developers aren't thinking about them yet.

tips.short: Switch models mid-session without starting over.
tips.heading: /model command
tips.what: /model lists available models and lets you switch. Haiku for fast/cheap iteration; Sonnet for most tasks; Opus for complex reasoning or extended thinking.
tips.when: You're doing quick file edits (switch to Haiku). You hit a hard reasoning problem (switch to Opus). You want to save on a long autonomous run.
tips.why: Using Opus for everything is expensive and slow. Using Haiku for everything misses nuance. Switching deliberately is a skill.

tips.short: Haiku for iteration, Sonnet for most work, Opus for hard problems.
tips.heading: Model selection by task type
tips.what: Haiku: fast file edits, simple transforms, quick lookups. Sonnet: most coding tasks, analysis, multi-step reasoning. Opus: architecture decisions, complex debugging, extended thinking, adversarial review.
tips.when: Every time you start a new task. The model matters more than most prompt changes.
tips.why: Wrong model = wrong speed/quality trade-off. Haiku gives wrong answers on hard problems. Opus is 5-10x more expensive than needed for simple tasks.

tips.short: Diagnose Claude Code setup issues automatically.
tips.heading: /doctor command
tips.what: /doctor checks your Claude Code installation, API keys, MCP connections, and common configuration issues. Tells you what's broken and how to fix it.
tips.when: First time setting up on a new machine. When something stops working and you're not sure what changed.
tips.why: Setup issues are frustrating to diagnose manually. /doctor surfaces them in seconds.

tips.short: Search the web inline without leaving the conversation.
tips.heading: @web for inline search
tips.what: Type @web followed by your query. Claude searches the web and includes current results in its response.
tips.when: You need current information (library docs, API changes, recent events). When Claude's training data might be outdated.
tips.why: Claude's knowledge has a cutoff. @web extends it to today without breaking your workflow.

tips.short: Connect Claude to external APIs and tools directly.
tips.heading: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
tips.what: MCP servers give Claude tools: search Airtable, query Slack, browse the web, run SQL, call internal APIs. Set them up in settings and Claude uses them automatically.
tips.when: You find yourself copy-pasting data between Claude and another tool. You want Claude to take action in an external system.
tips.why: Every manual copy-paste is a workflow you can eliminate. MCP makes Claude an active participant in your toolchain, not just a text box.

tips.short: Make Claude plan and show you the plan before writing any code.
tips.heading: Plan mode
tips.what: In plan mode, Claude proposes an approach and waits for your approval before doing anything. Use /plan to enter it.
tips.when: Large tasks where the wrong approach means hours of rework. Architecture changes. Anything touching production data.
tips.why: A 5-minute plan review can save a 2-hour wrong-direction execution.

tips.short: Turn off permission prompts for long autonomous runs.
tips.heading: Auto-accept mode
tips.what: Shift+Tab toggles auto-accept. Claude runs tool calls (file edits, terminal commands) without pausing to ask. Toggle it back off when you want to review each step.
tips.when: Big refactors, scaffolding, multi-step pipelines where individual steps are low-risk.
tips.why: If you're doing a 20-file refactor and Claude asks permission on every write, you'll lose your mind. Toggle this on, go do something else, come back.

tips.short: Define custom tools so Claude can take action in your systems.
tips.heading: Custom tool definitions
tips.what: Using the Anthropic API directly, you can define tools with JSON schemas. Claude decides when to call them and what arguments to pass. You handle the execution.
tips.when: Building agentic workflows. When you need Claude to call internal APIs, query databases, or trigger external actions.
tips.why: Pre-built MCP servers cover common tools. Custom tools cover your specific systems. The combination makes Claude able to act in almost any environment.

tips.short: Build your own MCP server to give Claude access to internal tools.
tips.heading: Custom MCP server development
tips.what: The mcp-developer skill helps you build production MCP servers: auth handling, error handling, tool definitions, and deployment. Any internal API can become a Claude tool.
tips.when: You have internal APIs, databases, or tools that Claude should be able to use directly. Jira, internal search, proprietary data sources.
tips.why: The productivity gain from giving Claude direct access to your internal tools is significant. Manual bridging doesn't scale.

tips.short: Track token costs across sessions — it adds up faster than you expect.
tips.heading: Cost awareness
tips.what: Use /cost to see token usage for the current session. Factor model choice, context length, and tool call frequency into how you structure workflows.
tips.when: Running long autonomous workflows. Before putting a workflow into production. When your bill looks higher than expected.
tips.why: A 10-minute Opus session can cost as much as 10 hours of Haiku. Cost awareness shapes how you architect workflows.

tips.short: Run typecheck or tests automatically after every file edit.
tips.heading: PostToolUse hooks
tips.what: PostToolUse hooks fire a shell command after every Write tool call. Claude sees the output and fixes errors before moving on. Set up in settings.json.
tips.when: Any TypeScript or Python project. Add a hook for tsc or pytest and you stop discovering errors at the end.
tips.why: Without hooks, you find type errors when the task is done. With hooks, Claude fixes them as it goes.

tips.short: Save your working state so you can restore it if things go wrong.
tips.heading: Checkpoint skill
tips.what: The checkpoint skill saves git state, current decisions, and remaining work to a recovery file. Run it before any risky operation.
tips.when: Before large refactors. Before running an autonomous sequence. Any time you might want to roll back to a known good state.
tips.why: Recovery from a bad autonomous run is much faster when you have a clear restore point.

tips.short: Initialize a project with roadmap, milestones, and decisions from scratch.
tips.heading: /gsd-new-project skill
tips.what: Runs deep context gathering, creates a PROJECT.md with goals, constraints, and architecture decisions, and sets up a full .planning/ directory structure.
tips.when: Starting a new project from scratch. When an existing project lacks documented decisions and structure.
tips.why: Projects without upfront structure accumulate undocumented decisions that slow down every future session.

tips.short: Write a detailed implementation plan before touching code.
tips.heading: Writing plans skill
tips.what: The writing-plans skill creates a step-by-step implementation plan saved to .plans/. Each step is atomic, has clear success criteria, and accounts for dependencies.
tips.when: Before any task larger than a single-file change. Before you'd normally just start coding.
tips.why: Plans catch design problems before they become code problems. A good plan also makes execution much faster.

tips.short: Execute a saved plan with checkpoints and review gates.
tips.heading: Executing plans skill
tips.what: The executing-plans skill loads a .plans/ file and works through it step by step, committing after each atomic task and pausing at review gates.
tips.when: After you've written and approved a plan. For long multi-step implementations where you want clear progress tracking.
tips.why: Execution without a plan drifts. Execution against a plan stays on track and makes it obvious when something is deviating.

tips.short: Tests, review, version bump, changelog, PR — one command.
tips.heading: Ship skill
tips.what: The ship skill runs the full pre-ship sequence: tests pass, code review, version bump, CHANGELOG update, PR creation. Guided, not autonomous.
tips.when: When you're done implementing and ready to merge. Replaces manually tracking a pre-ship checklist.
tips.why: Pre-ship checklists get skipped when you're busy. Automation doesn't.

tips.short: Create a phase plan with verification loop before any execution.
tips.heading: /gsd-plan-phase skill
tips.what: Creates a detailed PLAN.md for a GSD phase with task breakdown, dependency analysis, and goal-backward verification. Runs a checker agent before handing off to execution.
tips.when: Starting a new development phase within a project. Before any significant block of implementation work.
tips.why: Execution without a verified plan often completes the wrong thing. The verification loop catches misalignment before code is written.

tips.short: Work on multiple branches simultaneously in isolated directories.
tips.heading: Git worktrees skill
tips.what: The using-git-worktrees skill creates isolated working directories for each feature branch. Claude Code can run in multiple worktrees simultaneously without conflicts.
tips.when: Working on multiple features in parallel. When you want to keep main always clean while experimenting on a branch.
tips.why: Stashing and context-switching between branches is slow and error-prone. Worktrees eliminate both.

tips.short: Run a full discuss to plan to execute to verify cycle unattended.
tips.heading: /gsd-autonomous skill
tips.what: Chains all GSD phase skills in sequence: discuss requirements, plan, execute with commits, verify against goals. Runs with minimal intervention.
tips.when: Well-defined tasks where you've already done similar work. When you want to walk away and come back to a completed feature.
tips.why: The overhead of running each phase manually adds up. Autonomous mode collapses it when the task is clear enough.

tips.short: Merge, wait for CI, verify production — fully automated.
tips.heading: Land and deploy skill
tips.what: The land-and-deploy skill merges a PR, watches CI, waits for the deploy to complete, then runs canary verification against production. Alerts if anything looks wrong.
tips.when: After a PR is approved and ready to merge. When you want confidence that production is healthy after a deploy.
tips.why: Merging and walking away is how broken deploys go unnoticed for hours. Land-and-deploy closes that gap.

tips.short: Fresh context, no pollution — use subagents for independent tasks.
tips.heading: What subagents are for
tips.what: Subagents are fresh Claude sessions dispatched for a specific task. They don't share context with the main session, which means no pollution — but also no shared state.
tips.when: Any task that doesn't depend on the current conversation. Research, file analysis, parallel implementation.
tips.why: Fresh context means focused attention. A subagent reading a file starts with no assumptions from your session.

tips.short: If two tasks don't depend on each other, run them at the same time.
tips.heading: Parallel execution basics
tips.what: When you have multiple independent tasks, dispatch them simultaneously rather than sequentially. Research X while also researching Y. Review file A while also reviewing file B.
tips.when: Anytime you catch yourself waiting for one thing to finish before starting another unrelated thing.
tips.why: Sequential execution of independent tasks is pure waste. Parallelism collapses wall-clock time.

tips.short: Dispatch multiple focused agents simultaneously with isolated context.
tips.heading: Parallel agents skill
tips.what: The dispatching-parallel-agents skill breaks a task into independent subtasks, dispatches them as isolated subagents simultaneously, then synthesizes results. Each agent starts fresh.
tips.when: Research across multiple sources. Code review across multiple files. Generating multiple design variants.
tips.why: One agent doing five things sequentially is 5x slower and accumulates context pollution. Five agents doing one thing each is faster and cleaner.

tips.short: One subagent per plan task, with two-stage spec and quality review.
tips.heading: Subagent-driven development
tips.what: The subagent-driven-development skill dispatches a fresh subagent for each plan task. Each subagent gets a two-stage review: does it match the spec? Does it meet quality standards?
tips.when: Large implementation projects where quality gates matter. When you want every task independently verified.
tips.why: A single long session accumulates fatigue and context drift. Fresh agents per task maintain consistent quality across the whole project.

tips.short: Run multiple parallel work streams with independent state.
tips.heading: /gsd-workstreams skill
tips.what: Creates isolated workstream environments for parallel tracks of work: feature A, feature B, and a hotfix can all be in-progress simultaneously with their own planning state.
tips.when: When you're juggling multiple concurrent features. When you need to context-switch between tracks without losing state.
tips.why: Parallel work without state management creates confusion about where each stream is. Workstreams give each track a clean home.

tips.short: Turn your codebase into a queryable knowledge graph.
tips.heading: Graphify skill
tips.what: The graphify skill analyzes your codebase, builds a knowledge graph with community detection, and produces an interactive HTML report showing relationships between modules, functions, and files.
tips.when: Onboarding to a complex codebase. Planning large refactors. When you want to understand blast radius before changing something.
tips.why: Large codebases are too connected to understand by reading files. A graph makes structure visible and relationships traceable.

tips.short: Command center for managing multiple active phases from one terminal.
tips.heading: /gsd-manager skill
tips.what: The gsd-manager skill gives you an interactive dashboard for all active phases: status, blockers, next actions, and dispatch controls. Run across all phases without switching contexts.
tips.when: Managing a project with 3+ concurrent phases. When you need a unified view of what's in progress.
tips.why: Checking status across multiple phases manually wastes time. gsd-manager collapses it into one view.

tips.short: Autonomous audit, classify, fix, test, commit pipeline.
tips.heading: /gsd-audit-fix skill
tips.what: Finds all outstanding issues in a phase (security, quality, test coverage), classifies by severity, fixes in priority order, runs tests, and commits each fix atomically.
tips.when: After a fast implementation sprint where quality was deferred. Before a release with known tech debt.
tips.why: Manual audit-fix cycles are interrupted and incomplete. Autonomous pipelines finish what they start.

tips.short: Subagents that need each other's outputs must share state explicitly.
tips.heading: State passing between agents
tips.what: When one subagent's output is needed by another, pass it explicitly (write to a file, return it in a structured format). Agents don't share a context window unless you architect that explicitly.
tips.when: Building pipelines where agent A produces input for agent B. Sequential workflows with handoffs.
tips.why: Assuming agents share context is the most common multi-agent bug. They don't. You have to wire it.

tips.short: Give another AI agent scoped browser access to collaborate.
tips.heading: Pair agent skill
tips.what: The pair-agent skill generates a setup key that gives another AI agent (Codex, Cursor, Gemini) scoped access to your browser session. Collaborate across AI tools on the same interface.
tips.when: When you want a second AI opinion on a UI or when you're using multiple AI tools on the same project.
tips.why: Different AI tools have different strengths. Sharing a browser session lets you route tasks to the right tool without manual context transfer.

tips.short: Update README, CHANGELOG, and docs after every ship — not someday.
tips.heading: Post-ship documentation
tips.what: The document-release skill syncs README, ARCHITECTURE.md, and CHANGELOG to reflect what actually shipped. Runs after every release.
tips.when: Immediately after merging a PR. Before asking anyone else to use or maintain what you built.
tips.why: Docs written after the fact are written by someone who already forgot the details. Docs written right after shipping are accurate.

tips.short: Route ideas, tasks, and notes before you lose them.
tips.heading: /gsd-capture skill
tips.what: The gsd-capture skill takes a raw idea or note and routes it to the right destination: backlog, seed list, decision log, or active phase. Stops ideas from living in random message threads.
tips.when: Any time you have an idea mid-task that you can't act on right now. At the end of a session.
tips.why: Ideas that don't get captured don't get acted on. Capture takes 30 seconds.

tips.short: Unified status: where am I, what's next, what's blocked.
tips.heading: /gsd-progress skill
tips.what: Shows current phase status, next actions, blockers, and percent complete across all active work. One command for full situational awareness.
tips.when: At the start of every session. When you're not sure what to work on next.
tips.why: Working without a status check means you might pick up the wrong thing. gsd-progress makes the right next action obvious.

tips.short: YC-style forcing questions to stress-test your idea before building it.
tips.heading: Office hours skill
tips.what: The office-hours skill channels a YC partner mode: sharp forcing questions about demand reality, assumptions, competition, and what problem is actually being solved.
tips.when: Before committing to building something. When you're not sure if an idea is worth pursuing. When you want your assumptions challenged.
tips.why: It's faster to kill a bad idea in 20 minutes of hard questions than after 3 weeks of building.

tips.short: 5-expert briefing on any topic across multiple perspectives.
tips.heading: Storm research skill
tips.what: The storm-research skill produces a structured briefing viewed through 5 expert lenses, with a contradiction map and peer review. Covers a topic from multiple angles simultaneously.
tips.when: Before making a decision that depends on understanding a domain you don't know well. Market research, technical landscape mapping, competitive analysis.
tips.why: Single-perspective research has blind spots. Five lenses expose trade-offs and contradictions you'd otherwise miss.

tips.short: Weekly engineering retrospective with trends tracked over time.
tips.heading: Retro skill
tips.what: The retro skill produces a weekly engineering retrospective: velocity, blockers, wins, learnings, and action items. Tracks trends across weeks.
tips.when: End of every sprint or week. Before planning the next period.
tips.why: Teams that don't retrospect repeat the same problems. Written retros that track trends surface patterns that weekly conversations miss.

tips.short: Human-readable milestone summary for onboarding or stakeholder updates.
tips.heading: /gsd-milestone-summary skill
tips.what: Generates a clean summary of what was built in a milestone: goals, outcomes, decisions, and what's next. Readable by non-engineers.
tips.when: After completing a milestone. When onboarding someone new to the project. When reporting to stakeholders.
tips.why: Technical artifacts (PLAN.md, git log) aren't readable by stakeholders. A summary bridges the gap without losing accuracy.

tips.short: Triage GitHub issues and PRs systematically — don't let the queue grow.
tips.heading: /gsd-inbox skill
tips.what: The gsd-inbox skill reviews open GitHub issues and PRs, categorizes them (bug/feature/question/wontfix), and surfaces the ones that need action.
tips.when: Weekly issue triage. When your issue backlog has grown beyond what you track mentally.
tips.why: Unmanaged issue queues lose trust. Regular triage keeps the signal-to-noise ratio high.

tips.short: CEO, design, eng, and DX review before any implementation.
tips.heading: Autoplan — full pre-implementation review
tips.what: The autoplan skill runs all four review lenses sequentially (CEO/product, design, engineering, developer experience) with auto-decisions, ending at one approval gate.
tips.when: Before any significant feature. When you want maximum pre-build confidence that you're building the right thing, the right way.
tips.why: Most implementation mistakes are identifiable before a line of code is written. Four lenses catch four different failure modes.

tips.short: Challenge scope, raise ambition, find the 10-star version.
tips.heading: CEO review skill
tips.what: The plan-ceo-review skill reads your plan from a founder/CEO perspective: Is this the right problem? Is the scope too small? What's the 10-star version? What would a competitor do differently?
tips.when: Before locking in a feature roadmap. When you suspect you're solving the symptom, not the problem.
tips.why: Engineering reviews catch implementation problems. CEO review catches the wrong problem being solved well.
